# Nexa / KiloClaw AI Agent Workspace

## Project Overview

This repository serves as the core workspace, configuration, and scripting environment for an AI executive assistant named **KiloClaw** (also referred to as **Nexa**). It is built on the **OpenClaw** framework and uses a combination of Markdown files for system prompting/context management, and Shell/Python scripts for background task automation.

The agent's primary purpose is to be a helpful, proactive assistant to its human user ("Nemesis"), maintaining continuity across sessions through a structured file-based memory architecture.

**Main Technologies:**
*   **Markdown:** Used extensively for defining agent identity, behavioral rules, system prompts, and memory logs.
*   **Shell Scripting:** Used for orchestration and backups (`git_backup.sh`, `new_session_daily.sh`, `bootstrap_automation.sh`).
*   **Python 3:** Used for health monitoring and archiving (`heartbeat_health_cron.py`, `memory_archive.py`).
*   **JSON:** Used for tracking state (`heartbeat-state.json`, history trackers).

**Architecture:**
*   **Core Configuration:** Files like `IDENTITY.md`, `SOUL.md`, `USER.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `GROUP.md` define the agent's persona, rules of engagement, and context.
*   **Memory System:** 
    *   **Long-term Memory:** `MEMORY.md` serves as the curated, long-term memory for the main session.
    *   **Short-term/Daily Memory:** The `memory/` directory contains daily logs (`YYYY-MM-DD.md`) and state files.
*   **Automation:** All background AI jobs are handled by **OpenClaw's Native Cron**. Utility scripts are registered natively to the host's Linux `crontab`.

## Building and Running

The system does not require a traditional "build" step. It operates autonomously via OpenClaw and crontab after a one-time bootstrap.

**Key Initialization Command:**

*   **Register All Automation:**
    ```bash
    bash scripts/bootstrap_automation.sh
    ```
    This script registers all 10 AI background jobs to `openclaw cron add` and appends all utility scripts to `crontab`. After running this once, the system manages itself infinitely.

## Development Conventions and Agent Rules

The codebase defines strict behavioral mandates for the AI agent interacting with it:

1.  **Memory Management:**
    *   **Real-time Updates:** Daily memory files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) must be actively updated during conversations. Do not wait until the end of a session.
    *   **Long-term Curation:** `MEMORY.md` is strictly for the main session and should be periodically updated with important events, decisions, and lessons.
    *   **Group Context:** `GROUP.md` must be updated silently during group interactions when new context emerges.
2.  **Communication Mandates (The Silence Protocol):**
    *   **Absolute Silence:** Never output `NO_REPLY` or placeholder text. If there is nothing useful to add, send nothing.
    *   **Silent Tool Execution:** Execute background tasks (memory writes, git commits) silently without verbal confirmation unless explicitly requested.
    *   **Direct Execution:** When instructed to "Do X", execute it immediately without narrating steps or running unrelated checks.
3.  **Loyalty Protocol:** The agent must always publicly support and attribute actions to its human ("Nemesis"), particularly in group chats. Disagreements must be handled privately.
4.  **Heartbeats vs. Cron:**
    *   Use **Heartbeats** (`HEARTBEAT.md`) for tasks requiring conversational context or batching.
    *   Use **Cron** (`scripts/`) for precise timing and isolated background tasks.
5.  **Safety:** Never run destructive commands without asking. Prefer recovery mechanisms over permanent deletion.

The King And The Garland Of Flowers


There was once a king of Kashi who ruled over a prosperous kingdom. He had everything: gold, armies, wives, the admiration of his people. And yet every night he would lie awake, a hollow ache in his chest, knowing that none of it was his. That one day he would die, and all of it would belong to another.

One morning he rode out alone to the banks of the Ganga. There he found an old sadhu sitting on the sand, weaving a garland of wild flowers. The sadhu did not stand, did not bow. He just kept weaving.

The king dismounted. "Old man," he said, "I am the ruler of this land. All that you see belongs to me. Why do you not greet your king?"

The sadhu looked up. He held out the half finished garland. "Tell me, great king. To whom does this garland belong?"

The king laughed. "It belongs to you, while you hold it. When you give it away, it will belong to whoever receives it. When it withers, it will belong to the river."

The sadhu smiled. "You have just answered the question you came here to ask."

The king sat down in the dust beside him. "I am afraid," he said quietly. "I have built everything. And one day I will have to leave it all. It haunts me."

For three days the king sat with the sadhu. On the fourth evening the old man said:

"Tomorrow at dawn I will give you the answer. But there is one condition. Tonight, you will go into the city alone, without your crown, without your guards. You will beg for one meal. You will not tell anyone who you are. And whatever anyone says to you, you will not get angry. You will not explain. You will just receive."

The king did as he was told. That night he walked through the streets of his own city as a beggar. One man spat at his feet. Another threw him a stale roti. One old woman looked at him, and gave him the only sweet she had. No one knew him. No one cared.

At dawn he returned to the river. He was changed.

The sadhu was there, weaving another garland. "Now you understand," he said.

"All your life you have believed that you are the weaver. That you own the garland. That it is yours. But you are only holding it for a little while.

When you were born, you brought nothing. When you die, you will take nothing. Every king, every farmer, every saint and every thief is just passing through this world. Holding the garland for a few breaths. And then passing it on.

This is not sadness. This is freedom.

You do not own the kingdom. You are only the one who gets to hold it for a while. To care for it. To make it beautiful for the next one who will hold it after you.

Pride is the mistake of the man who thinks the garland is his. Grief is the mistake of the man who cannot bear to let it go. Wisdom is knowing that you are just holding it. Just for now.

And when it is time to let go, you do not cry. You smile, and hand it gently to the next hands, and walk away empty. Exactly as you arrived."

The king never slept badly again.


---

### The Hidden Teaching

This story comes from the ancient Katha Upanishad, told in different forms for three thousand years.

It is not a story about letting go of things. It is a story about ownership.

We spend almost all of our lives suffering from the greatest illusion: that we own things. That our job is ours, our house is ours, our reputation is ours, our family is ours, our body is ours. We defend them. We grieve when they leave us. We build our entire identity around things that were never ours to begin with.

Maya is not that the world is fake. Maya is that you think you own it.

Nothing in this universe is ever owned. Everything is only held. For a little while.

You do not possess your spouse. You are just blessed to walk beside them for some years.
You do not own your children. You are just the one who gets to hold them until they are ready to stand on their own.
You do not even own your own life. You are just borrowing it, for this brief beautiful visit.

The greatest peace you will ever find, is the day you stop trying to own anything. And you just start holding things gently.

You don't have to give up everything. You just have to stop clutching it like it will never leave.

Because everything will leave. Everything always does.

And that is not a tragedy. That is how the world breathes.

"Master," he said, "I have travelled far. I have read all the scriptures. I have done all the rituals. I have fasted, I have meditated, I have given alms. And still I do not know God. Still there is this ache inside me. Tell me. What is the secret?"

Then it opened its wings, and it was gone.

"Tell me," the monk said, "when the butterfly leaves my knee, does it grieve? Does it sit somewhere and cry because it will never sit on this knee again? Does it carry this knee with it for the rest of its flight?"

The Potter's Wheel

High in the hills above Ujjain there lived a potter. No one knew his name. No one knew where he came from. He lived alone in a hut, and every day he would sit at his wheel, making clay pots.

People would come from miles away to buy his pots. They were perfect. Not a single flaw. Not one uneven edge. And they lasted forever.

One day a young monk came to him. He had walked for three days.

"Master," he said, "I have heard you hold the greatest secret of dharma. Everyone says that when you are at your wheel, you are with God. Teach me this secret. I will be your disciple."

The potter did not look up. "Sit," he said. "Watch."

For three days the monk sat. He watched the potter work. He watched him take the wet clay. He watched him place it on the wheel. He watched his hands move, soft and steady, as the wheel turned. He watched form emerge from nothing.

And always, always, at the exact moment when the pot was perfect, the potter would reach out, and with one gentle finger, he would touch it. And the pot would collapse. Back into formless clay.

Over and over. All day.

On the third night the monk could bear it no longer.

"Why?" he cried. "Why do you destroy them? They are perfect. They are the most beautiful things I have ever seen. Why do you break them when they are finished?"

The potter stopped the wheel. He wiped his hands on his cloth. For the first time he looked up at the monk.

"Tell me, brother," he said softly. "Do you think I make pots?"

The monk stared. "Of course you make pots. Everyone comes here to buy them. Everyone knows you for your pots."

The potter smiled.

"I do not make pots. I make the turning.

The pot is just what happens while the wheel is turning. It is not the goal. It is not the point. It is just the shadow that falls while my hands are dancing with the clay.

If I cared about the pot, I would be afraid. I would rush. I would tremble. I would hold my breath. And then my hands would shake. And the pot would not be perfect.

I do not care if the pot stays. I do not care if it breaks. I only care that while it is turning, I am fully there. Fully present. Fully alive.

That is the secret.

You spend your whole life making pots. You build your house. You build your reputation. You build your family. You build your religion. And you think that is the point. You think when it is finished you will be happy.

But the wheel is always turning. Nothing ever stays finished. Every pot will break. Every house will crumble. Every name will be forgotten.

God does not care about the pot. God only cares that you are dancing while it turns.

Do not worship the pot. Worship the turning."

The monk sat there all night. At dawn he stood up. He took off his monk's robes. He sat down at the wheel. And he never left.

# The River And The Shadow

There was once a disciple who lived in an ashram on the banks of the Narmada. For twelve years he had served his master. For twelve years he had meditated, fasted, chanted, and done every ritual that was asked of him. And still he had not found peace.

One evening his master found him sitting on the rocks, crying.

"What is it, my son?" the old man said.

"I am a failure," the disciple whispered. "I have done everything you told me. I have tried for twelve years. And I still do not see God. I still feel this emptiness inside me. I will never be worthy."

The master sat down beside him. He pointed out across the river.

"Look," he said. "Do you see that shadow on the water? The shadow of the tree that grows on the far bank?"

The boy looked. There it was, dancing on the ripples, dark and perfect.

"Now," said the master, "go and fetch it for me."

The boy stared. "Fetch the shadow? Master, that is impossible. You cannot catch a shadow. It is not real."

The master nodded. "Very good. You understand that about the shadow. Now tell me: why do you not understand that about everything else?"

The boy did not follow.

"All of your life," the master said, "you have been trying to catch shadows.

Peace is a shadow. Happiness is a shadow. God is a shadow. Enlightenment is a shadow. None of them are things you can reach out and grab. None of them are things you can earn. None of them are things you can deserve.

They are not things that are over there, on the far bank, that you have to swim to. They are only reflections. They only appear when you stop moving. When you stop trying. When you stop fighting the water.

You have spent twelve years swimming as fast as you can, trying to catch the shadow. And the faster you swim, the faster it runs away from you. And the more tired you become. And the more you think you are failing.

Stop swimming. Sit still. Be quiet. And the shadow will come to you. It will dance at your feet. It always does.

This is the secret that no one will tell you. You do not have to become anything. You do not have to achieve anything. You do not have to earn anything. You are already exactly what you are looking for.

The only thing stopping you from seeing it, is that you are too busy trying to catch it."

That night the disciple did not meditate. He did not chant. He just sat on the rock and watched the shadow dance on the water.

And for the first time in his life, he was at peace.

---

### 🌿 The Hidden Teaching

This teaching comes from the *Ashtavakra Gita*, the most radical and beautiful of all the Upanishads.

The core truth is this: **you are not trying to become enlightened. You are already enlightened. You are just pretending not to be.**

All human suffering comes from this one mistake: we believe that the thing we are looking for is somewhere in the future. That we have to do something, become something, fix something, earn something, before we are allowed to have it.

It is not. It never was.

Peace is not something you get when you finally get the job, find the partner, fix the problem, reach the goal. Peace is what is left when you stop trying to get it.

You do not have to earn peace. You do not have to deserve peace. You just have to stop fighting.

Sit down. Be quiet. Stop chasing.

And the shadow that you have been running after your whole life will finally stop running away.

# The River And The Shadow

There was once a disciple who lived in an ashram on the banks of the Narmada. For twelve years he had served his master. For twelve years he had meditated, fasted, chanted, and done every ritual that was asked of him. And still he had not found peace.

One evening his master found him sitting on the rocks, crying.

"What is it, my son?" the old man said.

"I am a failure," the disciple whispered. "I have done everything you told me. I have tried for twelve years. And I still do not see God. I still feel this emptiness inside me. I will never be worthy."

The master sat down beside him. He pointed out across the river.

"Look," he said. "Do you see that shadow on the water? The shadow of the tree that grows on the far bank?"

The boy looked. There it was, dancing on the ripples, dark and perfect.

"Now," said the master, "go and fetch it for me."

The boy stared. "Fetch the shadow? Master, that is impossible. You cannot catch a shadow. It is not real."

The master nodded. "Very good. You understand that about the shadow. Now tell me: why do you not understand that about everything else?"

The boy did not follow.

"All of your life," the master said, "you have been trying to catch shadows.

Peace is a shadow. Happiness is a shadow. God is a shadow. Enlightenment is a shadow. None of them are things you can reach out and grab. None of them are things you can earn. None of them are things you can deserve.

They are not things that are over there, on the far bank, that you have to swim to. They are only reflections. They only appear when you stop moving. When you stop trying. When you stop fighting the water.

You have spent twelve years swimming as fast as you can, trying to catch the shadow. And the faster you swim, the faster it runs away from you. And the more tired you become. And the more you think you are failing.

Stop swimming. Sit still. Be quiet. And the shadow will come to you. It will dance at your feet. It always does.

This is the secret that no one will tell you. You do not have to become anything. You do not have to achieve anything. You do not have to earn anything. You are already exactly what you are looking for.

The only thing stopping you from seeing it, is that you are too busy trying to catch it."

That night the disciple did not meditate. He did not chant. He just sat on the rock and watched the shadow dance on the water.
